notes.anchor: on the fourth day before the Ides of May
notes.note: 12 May 60 BC. Romans dated by counting inclusively backward from the Kalends, Nones, or Ides; the Ides of May fell on the 15th, so the "fourth day before" (a.d. iv Id. Mai.) is the 12th.

notes.anchor: which you had given on the Ides of February
notes.note: 13 February 60 BC: the date Atticus handed his letter to the courier. In February the Ides fell on the 13th, not the 15th.

notes.anchor: And he of whom you write has nothing ample
notes.note: The unnamed man whose politics are "low-cast and popular" is Pompey, whose backing of the Flavian land bill (treated in the previous letter) Cicero deprecates while explaining why he has nonetheless allied with him.

An allusion to the proverb "Sparta is your lot; do it credit" (from Euripides): Cicero casts the optimate cause as the province assigned to him by fate, which he will not abandon even if abandoned by it.
Q. Lutatius Catulus (consul 78 BC), the acknowledged leader of the senatorial conservatives (optimates), had died in 61 or 60 BC, leaving Cicero's "aristocratic course" without a senior champion.
Rhinthon of Tarentum, a comic poet of the early third century BC; Cicero quotes (in Greek) his line that some men count for nothing and others care for nothing, a verdict on the inert nobility.
The piscinarii again: the wealthy, do-nothing nobles (Hortensius, Lucullus and the like) more devoted to their luxury fish-ponds than to the state, whose jealousy of Cicero he postpones discussing.

notes.anchor: the back-bench senators ran in haste
notes.note: The pedarii were senators who had not held curule office and so could only vote with their feet by crossing the floor, not speak; Cicero blames the unwelcome decree on the Sicyonians' debt on their unconsidered rush to one side.

notes.anchor: He wishes, I take it, to triumph
notes.note: The consul Metellus Celer, holding Cisalpine Gaul, would lose his chance of a triumph if peace broke out there before he could win a victory; hence his reluctance to welcome news of calm in Gaul.
L. Afranius (consul 60 BC), Pompey's man, whose consulship Cicero dismisses as so feeble that it is merely a "black eye" (Greek hypopion) on Pompey's reputation rather than a real magistracy.
"Magnus" (the Great) is Pompey's cognomen; Cicero puns that Afranius's incompetent consulship, secured by Pompey's bribery, is a disfiguring bruise on Pompey himself.

notes.anchor: I have sent you my consulship completed in Greek
notes.note: The Greek prose memoir of his consulship, again (see Att. 1.19.10); Cicero jokes that Atticus, as a connoisseur of Greek, will be jealous of so good a Greek work by a Roman.

The lex Cincia de donis et muneribus (204 BC) restricted gifts, especially to advocates; Cicero is reassured by Atticus's agent Cincius that the law permits him to accept the gift of Servius Claudius's library.
Scida: a single sheet or strip of papyrus. Cicero begs Atticus to mobilize his whole household so that none of the inherited library's rolls is lost in transit.
